career_path.mentor: N/A — alma mater coaching stint post-playing career
John Elway (GM), Matt Russell (Director of Player Personnel)

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: George Paton — worked directly with Paton on all personnel decisions from 2021-2024. Paton called Mougey 'one of the better evaluators I've been around.' Paton's philosophy: BPA-leaning, elite collegiate scouting focus, value mid-round production, build through the draft at premium positions.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Elway tree (2012–2020) → Paton tree (2021–2024). Elway's influence: aggressive roster-building mentality ('win now and from now on'), willingness to make big moves. Paton's influence: disciplined collegiate scouting, finding Day 2/3 value, patient roster-building, BPA over need at premium picks (e.g., Surtain over QB need). Paton himself came from the Rick Spielman/Minnesota Vikings tree (Bears → Dolphins → Vikings), which emphasized analytics-augmented scouting, trading back for value, and drafting high-floor players. Spielman is now Mougey's senior advisor with the Jets, creating a direct pipeline of that philosophy.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Mougey's midseason 2025 trade deadline activity — dealing Sauce Gardner and Quinnen Williams for draft capital — was more aggressive than Paton's typical approach. Paton's one major aggressive move (Russell Wilson trade) was widely seen as a mistake. Mougey appears more willing to make blockbuster trades to reshape roster than his mentor. His stated philosophy of 'aggressive but calculated' tracks with Elway's boldness filtered through Paton's analytical caution.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Only 1 draft as Jets GM. Lineage weight is high. Paton/Elway/Spielman DNA is the primary behavioral model. Will decrease to 0.50 after 2026 draft and 0.30 after 2027 draft.

key_influencers.name: Aaron Glenn
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Former CB — values secondary talent highly. Ran man-heavy, blitz-heavy defense in Detroit. Prefers 4-3 base with hybrid fronts. Wants physical, violent team that can run the ball. Glenn was 'right there with Mougey' on trade deadline decisions. Both report individually to ownership. Glenn is calling the defense in 2026, so his scheme preferences directly dictate defensive personnel needs.

key_influencers.name: Rick Spielman
key_influencers.title: Senior Football Advisor
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Vikings GM (2012-2021). Known for aggressive first-round trading, drafting high-floor players, strong mid-round hit rate. Spielman's Vikings were known for finding value at EDGE (Danielle Hunter R3), LB (Eric Kendricks R2), and WR (Adam Thielen UDFA, Justin Jefferson R1). Assists Mougey on talent evaluation and roster construction but does not have final say.

key_influencers.name: Frank Reich
key_influencers.title: Offensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: Veteran offensive mind. System will feature running game and play-action passing. Physical, Northeast-weather-appropriate offense. Values pass protection and QB development.

key_influencers.name: Woody Johnson
key_influencers.title: Chairman/CEO
key_influencers.known_biases: Both HC and GM report directly to Johnson. Has history of owner interference — fired Joe Douglas and Robert Saleh midseason in 2024. Praised Mougey publicly but organizational structure creates potential power struggle. Called Mougey the 'sidekick' at introductory presser, suggesting Glenn was initially viewed as the primary hire.

front_office.power_dynamics: Both Glenn and Mougey report individually to ownership, which historically has created friction in Jets organizations. However, reports from the 2025 trade deadline and 2026 combine indicate Mougey is emerging as the more influential figure. Glenn was initially the 'Batman' but Mougey's strong trade deadline performance has shifted perceptions. Glenn's 3-14 record in year one puts him on a hotter seat than Mougey. Mougey controls the draft board and roster decisions; Glenn has significant input on scheme fit and defensive personnel.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Johnson has a documented history of meddling. He fired the previous GM and HC midseason. The dual-reporting structure to ownership creates ambiguity. However, Johnson has publicly backed both Glenn and Mougey through the rebuild.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Draft, develop, keep our own, and be disciplined and strategic in free agency. Build from the inside out — strong in the trenches.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: In his one draft, Mougey selected OT at 7 (clear need), TE at 42 (BPA-leaning — addressed future need over immediate holes), CB at 73 (need — secondary depleted), then speed WR, S, LB, and EDGE on Day 3 to fill roster gaps. The Membou pick at 7 was the 'meat-and-potatoes choice' over the 'glitzy' Tyler Warren pick, per ESPN, suggesting need and scheme fit slightly outweighed pure BPA. Glenn explicitly said he wanted to 'build the team from the inside out' like the Lions, and compared Membou to Penei Sewell. Paton's influence visible: taking the 'cleanest player' (Surtain over QB need in 2021) maps to Mougey's OT-over-TE decision.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims to be aggressive but calculated. Actual behavior in 2025 draft was conservative — no trades up in Round 1, took the safe OL pick. Day 3 showed more aggression — traded up twice for specific targets (Moore at 130, Baron at 176). This mirrors Paton's Broncos approach: conservative in Round 1, more active on Day 2-3.

trade_triggers: Quarterback availability — expressed willingness to explore trading up to No. 1 for Mendoza but acknowledged it was unlikely. QB need is the clear trade-up trigger.
Specific defensive targets on Day 3 — traded up twice on Day 3 of 2025 draft for Malachi Moore and Tyler Baron, both scheme-fit defenders.

Asset accumulation for rebuild — blockbuster midseason trades of Sauce Gardner (two 1sts + WR) and Quinnen Williams (1st + 2nd + player) show willingness to flip established stars for future draft capital when value is right.
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Aggressively accumulates picks. Currently holds two 1st-round picks (No. 2 and No. 16) and four picks in top 45 for 2026. Has five first-round picks over next two drafts. Structured Gardner's extension specifically to maintain trade flexibility. Clearly values future draft capital over present star power when team is not competitive. Paton influence: 'more darts' approach to drafting.

position_avoidance: RB in early rounds — signed Justin Fields to a FA deal and franchise-tagged Breece Hall rather than drafting the position. Paton's Broncos rarely invested early RB capital after the Javonte Williams R2 pick in 2021.
position_premium: OT — first-ever pick was an OT at 7 overall. Glenn's 'build from the inside out' philosophy and desire to emulate the Lions' dominant OL make this the top priority.
EDGE — traded Jermaine Johnson in 2026 offseason, creating clear need. Mock drafts project EDGE at No. 2 overall. Glenn's 4-3 defense requires premium edge talent.
CB — Glenn is a former CB. His Detroit defenses played league-high levels of man coverage. Drafted Azareye'h Thomas (long press CB) in Round 3 of first draft.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Long, versatile edge defender who can set the edge in the run game and rush the passer with length and power. Must be able to play in a 4-3 as a hand-in-dirt DE or stand up in sub-packages. Glenn's Detroit defense valued EDGE players who were disciplined run defenders first with pass-rush upside. Tyler Baron (6-5, 258) — the one EDGE Mougey has drafted — fits this mold: long, good motor, technically refined, sets the edge well, wins with leverage and hand usage rather than pure speed. Broncos influence: Nik Bonitto (6-3, 248, R2 2022) and Jonah Elliss (6-4, 250, R3 2024) were similar long, high-motor edge players drafted by the Paton/Mougey regime.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches — Baron measured 33.125 inches. Bonitto had 32.5-inch arms. Length clearly valued.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump (35+ preferred as explosiveness proxy) and broad jump (10-0+). Baron posted 35.5-inch vertical and 10-1 broad.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Baron ran 4.62. Not seeking elite 40 times but adequate closing speed.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Length and leverage at the point of attack. Hand usage and technique over pure athleticism.
most_valued_traits: Run defense discipline and edge-setting ability
Motor and effort consistency — Baron described as 'high motor'
Versatility to play hand-in-dirt or stand up
Technical hand usage and counter moves
Length usage to keep blockers at bay
least_important_traits: Elite bend/flexibility — Baron lacks elite bend but compensates with technique
Top-end 40 speed — not seeking track-star speed rushers
deal_breakers: Poor run defense — Glenn's Detroit defense prioritized run stopping
Lack of motor or effort inconsistency
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Versatility to play multiple defensive front alignments. Baron's ability to stand up or put hand in dirt was specifically noted.

college_production.production_floor: 5+ sacks in final college season for Day 1-2 picks. Baron had 5.5 sacks and 11 TFLs at Miami.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending production and multi-year experience. Baron had 12.5 career sacks over 5 years at Tennessee before 5.5 at Miami.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — adjusts for scheme and usage. Baron's rotation at Tennessee contextualized.

examples: 2025 R5 No. 176 — Tyler Baron (Miami): 6-5, 258, 4.62 40, 33.125-inch arms, 35.5-inch vert. 5.5 sacks, 11 TFL in final year. Long, technically refined, high motor edge setter. Traded up 10 spots to acquire.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 1 EDGE drafted (Day 3). Supplemented by Broncos scouting tree — Paton/Mougey drafted Bonitto (R2, 2022), Jonathon Cooper (R7, 2021), Jonah Elliss (R3, 2024). All were long, high-motor, versatile edge players.

OT.archetype_summary: Massive, elite-athleticism offensive tackle who can dominate in both run blocking and pass protection. Must have rare movement skills for size. Membou (6-4, 332, 4.91 40) is the prototype: an athletic freak at 332 pounds with a 34-inch vertical and 9-7 broad jump. Glenn explicitly compared Membou to Penei Sewell and wants to replicate the Lions' dominant offensive line. Zone-scheme versatile with enough power to road-grade in the run game.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches — Membou's 33.5-inch arms are on the shorter side for tackles, suggesting arm length is NOT an absolute threshold if athleticism compensates
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (sub-5.00 for OT is elite), vertical jump (34+), broad jump (9-6+), bench press (30+). Membou: 4.91 40, 34-inch vert, 9-7 broad, 31 bench reps. 99th percentile burst score.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.00 40 strongly preferred. Membou's 4.91 at 332 pounds was best-in-class.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Athleticism and movement skills that allow him to reach-block, pull, and mirror speed rushers despite massive size.
most_valued_traits: Elite athleticism for size — Membou was on Feldman's Freaks List
Punishing run blocking with leg drive and heavy punch
Foot speed in pass protection to handle speed rushers
Mentality and demeanor — Glenn said Membou's mentality was 'exactly the same' as Sewell's
Versatility to play tackle or potentially kick inside
least_important_traits: Arm length — willing to take shorter arms if athleticism compensates
Polished technique day one — willing to develop raw athletic tackles
deal_breakers: Lack of athleticism — will not draft a non-moving OT regardless of size
Poor demeanor/mentality — Glenn specifically cited this as the #1 attraction
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing at OT weight. Membou was taken 7th overall partly because of his rare physical/athletic combination.

college_production.production_floor: Must be a multi-year starter. Membou started 30+ games across 3 seasons at Missouri.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending trajectory — Membou improved each year, earning All-SEC honors as a junior.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values SEC/Power conference competition. Membou faced elite pass rushers weekly.

examples: 2025 R1 No. 7 — Armand Membou (Missouri): 6-4, 332, 4.91 40, 33.5-inch arms, 34-inch vert, 9-7 broad, 31 bench. 3 career sacks allowed. Road-grader with elite athleticism. Glenn compared him to Penei Sewell.

OT.confidence_note: Only 1 OT drafted but it was the flagship pick of the regime. Clear philosophical statement about what they want: elite athletes on the OL.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile interior lineman who can pull and execute zone concepts. Must have the foot speed to reach second-level defenders. Inferred from OT pick (Membou could play guard) and Broncos tree: Quinn Meinerz (6-3, 320, Division III, R3 2021) was a Paton/Mougey pick — an athletic, nastily physical guard who fit zone concepts. Luke Wattenberg (C, R5 2022) valued versatility. Expect Mougey to seek guards who can move and have a nasty streak.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for lateral movement. Bench press for anchor strength. 40-yard dash as athleticism proxy.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.10 40 preferred for guards in zone scheme.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Lateral movement and ability to execute zone-blocking scheme while providing anchor in pass protection.
most_valued_traits: Athleticism and foot speed for zone-run concepts
Nasty demeanor and physicality at the point of attack
Versatility to play multiple interior positions
Anchor strength in pass protection
Intelligence and communication skills
least_important_traits: Elite size — will take undersized guards with movement skills
Pure power-scheme mauling if it comes at expense of mobility
deal_breakers: Inability to move laterally — zone scheme demands it
Poor pass protection — Frank Reich's offense will need reliable protection
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Athletic testing that indicates elite movement at guard weight.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Low penalty count valued.
college_production.production_trajectory: Experience at center or multiple positions adds value.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values competition level. SEC/Big Ten starters preferred.

examples: No IOL drafted by Mougey as Jets GM. Broncos tree reference: Quinn Meinerz (R3, 2021) — 6-3, 320, Division III prospect with elite athleticism and physicality. Became All-Pro guard. This is the archetype Mougey helped evaluate.

IOL.confidence_note: No IOL drafted as Jets GM. Profile inferred from Membou's athletic OL archetype and Broncos' Meinerz pick. IOL is a glaring 2026 need with Vera-Tucker and Simpson as free agents.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive interior presence who can penetrate as a pass rusher and hold the point against the run. Glenn's 4-3 defense needs a 3-technique who can collapse the pocket and a nose/1-technique who can eat blocks. Mougey traded Quinnen Williams (elite pass-rushing DT) and acquired Mazi Smith (run-stuffing nose) in return, suggesting the regime values different DT archetypes for different roles. The Broncos under Paton/Mougey signed Zach Allen (6-5, 285, versatile DT who registered 8.5 sacks in 2024) — a high-motor, versatile interior player. Expect Mougey to target DT in 2026 draft given Quinnen Williams is gone.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches for 3-tech to play with length
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness (10-yard split) for 3-tech. Bench press for nose tackles. Vertical jump as explosiveness proxy.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.90 for 3-tech. Less important for nose.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior disruption through quickness and hand usage at 3-tech. Space-eating and block absorption at nose.
most_valued_traits: Pass-rush ability from interior — Glenn needs pocket collapse
Versatility to play multiple alignments along the DL
High motor and effort on every snap
Run defense as baseline competency
Hand technique to disengage from blocks
least_important_traits: Pure size/mass at 3-tech — will take undersized penetrators
Two-gap ability — Glenn's scheme is more one-gap penetrating
deal_breakers: One-dimensional run-only DT without pass-rush upside at 3-tech
Effort/motor concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite pass-rush win rate from interior. Interior pressure is most valuable DT trait in modern football.

college_production.production_floor: 3+ sacks for 3-tech prospects in rounds 1-2. TFL production more important than raw sack totals for DTs.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for double-team rates and scheme usage.

examples: No DT drafted by Mougey as Jets GM. Traded away Quinnen Williams (3x Pro Bowl DT) for draft capital — signaling willingness to replace via draft. Acquired Mazi Smith (run-stuffer) and T'Vondre Sweat (trade from Titans). Broncos signed Zach Allen (versatile, high-motor DT) under Mougey's tenure — that's the 3-tech archetype.

DT.confidence_note: No DTs drafted. Profile inferred from trade activity and Broncos tree. DT is a top-3 2026 draft need with Quinnen Williams gone. Multiple mocks project DT in Round 1 (No. 16).

CB.archetype_summary: Long, physical press-man cornerback with length to disrupt at the line and at the catch point. Glenn's defensive identity in Detroit was man-coverage heavy with league-high blitz rates — this demands corners who can hold up in single coverage. Azareye'h Thomas (6-1.5, 197, 32-3/8 arms) is the prototype: long, physical, excellent in press coverage, uses length to jam receivers and disrupt timing routes. Glenn played CB for 15 years and was a technician — he values technique and competitive toughness over pure speed.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31.5 inches — Thomas had 32-3/8 inch arms (5th longest at 2025 combine). Length is clearly a premium trait.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Broad jump (10-0+). Thomas posted 10-2. On-field agility and hip fluidity more important than raw 40 time.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred but not an absolute cutoff. Thomas did not run the 40 at the combine; pro day 40 around 4.58. Film speed and competitive speed matter more than timed speed to this staff.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Press-man — Glenn ran league-high man coverage rates in Detroit. This is the scheme identity.
most_valued_traits: Length and disruption at the line of scrimmage — Thomas excels at jamming receivers and disrupting timing routes
Competitive toughness and physicality — willingness to tackle, play the run, engage physically
Ball skills at the catch point — using length and body control to contest
Hip fluidity and change of direction — must mirror routes in man coverage
Special teams value — Glenn noted Moore's special teams background as a plus
least_important_traits: Interception production — Thomas had only 2 INTs in 3 seasons; Glenn values coverage disruption over ball-hawking numbers
Zone coverage instincts — scheme is man-first
deal_breakers: Inability to play press coverage — zone-only corners don't fit Glenn's scheme
Lack of competitive toughness or tackling willingness
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite length with press-man technique. Thomas was taken in Round 3 despite not running the 40 at the combine — the length and film were enough.

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor — Thomas had modest counting stats (52 tackles, 4 PBU, 1 INT in 2024) but elite coverage metrics (50.2 passer rating when targeted, 141 yards allowed in 357 coverage snaps).
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year starters but Thomas was technically a 1-year starter. Ascending trajectory valued.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values advanced metrics (passer rating allowed, yards per coverage snap) over raw stats.

examples: 2025 R3 No. 73 — Azareye'h Thomas (Florida State): 6-1.5, 197, 32-3/8 arms, 10-0 hands (biggest at combine for CB), 10-2 broad jump. Long press corner. 19 PBU in career. Only 2 INTs but elite coverage metrics. Did not run 40 at combine.

CB.confidence_note: 1 CB drafted but Glenn's Detroit track record provides strong supplementary signal. Glenn played man coverage at league-high rates and specifically stressed height at CB when discussing Sauce Gardner (6-3) and Brandon Stephens (6-1).

S.archetype_summary: Physical, tone-setting safety who can play in the box, cover underneath zones, and contribute on special teams. Malachi Moore (Alabama) was drafted as a 4th-round nickel/safety hybrid — a physical, instinctive player who 'hits you and you feel it,' per analysts. Glenn's Lions defense valued versatile safeties who could play multiple roles. The archetype is a do-everything defensive back: coverage ability, physicality against the run, and leadership/communication skills.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear minimum — Moore's arms not a distinguishing feature
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for change of direction. Physicality and tackling tested on film.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Adequate range speed — not seeking track-star speed. Moore was noted for 'lack of foot speed' but compensated with instincts and physicality.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Hybrid — can play nickel/star, strong safety, or single-high depending on personnel package. Versatility is the defining trait.
most_valued_traits: Physicality and tone-setting hitting ability — Moore 'plays with a tenacity of physicality'
Instincts and ball-tracking ability — great at diagnosing plays pre-snap
Versatility to play multiple secondary positions — Moore played nickel ('star') for 4 years then safety
Special teams ability — Moore was a starter on Alabama's special teams units
Communication and leadership — Glenn praised Jamien Sherwood as a 'true quarterback of the defense'
least_important_traits: Elite deep-range speed — willing to sacrifice range for physicality
Prototype single-high free safety skills — values box/hybrid safeties more
deal_breakers: Pure coverage-only safety without physicality or willingness to tackle
Inability to play multiple secondary spots
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite instincts and versatility. Traded up 15 spots to grab Moore specifically.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Moore was a 5-year player and 4-year starter at Alabama.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values experience and captaincy. Moore was a two-time team captain.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values SEC competition and leadership credentials.

examples: 2025 R4 No. 130 — Malachi Moore (Alabama): ~5-11, ~200, 4-year starter. First-team All-SEC in 2024. 70 tackles, 3 TFL, 2 INT, 8 PBU in final year. Traded up 15 spots. Versatile — played nickel and safety. Glenn/Mougey specifically sought his 'tenacity of physicality.'

S.confidence_note: 1 safety drafted, but the trade-up signals strong preference. Glenn's Detroit defenses heavily valued versatile secondary pieces like Brian Branch (nickel/safety hybrid). Mougey compared the Moore pick to Branch.

LB.archetype_summary: Rangy, instinctive linebacker who can cover, blitz, and play sideline-to-sideline. Francisco Mauigoa (Miami) was drafted as a 5th-round three-down linebacker with coverage chops, blitz timing, and run-stuffing ability. Glenn's 4-3 defense in Detroit valued linebackers like Alex Anzalone — not elite athletes but smart, instinctive, every-down defenders who could communicate and run the defense. Mougey appears to target LBs who can do multiple things rather than specialists.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold — flexible at LB
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (sub-4.65 preferred for range). Short shuttle for lateral movement. Mauigoa ran 4.60.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Must have sideline-to-sideline range.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Instinctive play diagnosis and three-down versatility.
most_valued_traits: Sideline-to-sideline range and pursuit speed
Blitz timing and ability to pressure from the LB level — Mauigoa had 13 career sacks
Instincts and play recognition — must diagnose quickly
Tackling reliability — Glenn values sure tacklers
Three-down ability — must stay on the field in passing situations
least_important_traits: Elite size/mass — willing to take undersized LBs who move well
Pure run-stuffing without coverage ability
deal_breakers: Coverage liability — cannot be a two-down player only
Missed tackles and inconsistent effort
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Blitz production combined with coverage versatility. Mauigoa's 35 career TFLs and 13 sacks from the LB position stood out.

college_production.production_floor: 60+ tackles in final season for mid-round picks. Mauigoa had 96 tackles in his final year at Miami.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending production and multi-school experience accepted. Mauigoa transferred from Washington State to Miami and improved.

examples: 2025 R5 No. 162 — Francisco Mauigoa (Miami): ~6-2, ~230, 4.60 40. 255 career tackles, 35 TFL, 13 sacks, 3 INTs across 4 seasons at Wash. State and Miami. Two-time All-ACC. Three-down versatile LB with blitz ability.

LB.confidence_note: 1 LB drafted on Day 3. Profile supplemented by Glenn's Detroit LB usage (Anzalone, Malcolm Rodriguez) which emphasized intelligence and versatility over pure athleticism.

WR.archetype_summary: Speed-first deep threat with explosive big-play ability. Arian Smith (Georgia) was drafted as a 4th-round pick: a world-class sprinter (4.36 40) with track-and-field background who can take the top off defenses. The Jets receiving corps 'needed a jolt of speed,' per ESPN. This was a traits-over-production bet — Smith had only 48 catches in his final season with 10 drops. The Broncos' Paton/Mougey regime also made similar bets: Marvin Mims Jr. (R2 2023, 5-11, 183, 4.38 40) was an explosive speedster. Expect Mougey to value explosive athleticism and YAC ability at WR.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-2 — flexible, no strong size preference based on 1 pick
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (speed is the primary filter), vertical jump (explosiveness), broad jump. Smith: 4.36 40, 38-inch vert, 10-7 broad, 6.82 3-cone.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.45 or faster strongly preferred. Smith ran 4.36. Speed is the differentiating trait.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Explosive speed that forces defensive adjustment. Deep threat ability.
most_valued_traits: Elite straight-line speed — Smith was the fastest player in the draft class
Big-play ability and yards-per-catch average — Smith averaged 17.0 YPC in final season
Ability to stretch the field vertically and create fear in defensive playcalling
Track-and-field background as athleticism proxy
Special teams value as returner/gunner
least_important_traits: Volume production — Smith had only 48 catches in his best season; production was not the driver
Contested catch ability — Smith is a slim 179 pounds, not a contested-catch WR
deal_breakers: Slow speed — will not draft a WR who cannot separate with athleticism
Cannot overcome drop issues if speed ceiling isn't special
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: World-class speed. Smith's 4.36 and track background were the primary selling points despite concerning drop issues (10 drops in 2024).

college_production.production_floor: Low — Smith had only 48 catches and 817 yards in his best season, with 10 drops. Mougey clearly values traits over production at WR. This is a departure from some GMs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Late breakout accepted — Smith didn't produce until his 5th-year senior season.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Smith played in a loaded Georgia offense with multiple weapons. Context adjusted production.

examples: 2025 R4 No. 110 — Arian Smith (Georgia): 6-0, 179, 4.36 40, 38-inch vert, 10-7 broad, 31-inch arms. 48 catches, 817 yards, 4 TD in 2024. 10 drops. World-class sprinter with track background. Deep threat archetype. Traits-over-production pick.

WR.confidence_note: 1 WR drafted on Day 3. May not represent Round 1-2 WR preferences. Broncos tree suggests Mims-like speed WR profile but could also value route runners in earlier rounds. Need more data.

TE.archetype_summary: Athletic, receiving-first tight end with NFL bloodlines and complete skill set. Mason Taylor (LSU, 6-5, 251) was the 2nd-round pick: a three-year starter with 129 career receptions who Mougey called 'young, athletic, really good ball skills, instinctive, has versatility.' Glenn said Taylor 'could do it all — block, be a receiving threat.' This is a modern TE who can line up inline, in the slot, or flexed out. Not a blocking-only TE. The 96th-percentile separation rate suggests they value route running and ability to find soft spots in coverage.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (4.65 or faster for receiving TE), 3-cone (sub-7.10 for route-running ability), bench press (functional blocking strength). Taylor: 4.65 40, 7.06 3-cone, 28 bench reps (all pro day numbers).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.70 or faster. Taylor ran 4.65. Must be able to threaten vertically and separate from LBs.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Receiving ability — soft hands, route running, ability to find soft spots in zone coverage.
most_valued_traits: Ball skills and natural hands — Taylor described as having 'soft but strong hands'
Route running and spatial awareness against zone coverage — 96th percentile separation rate
Versatility to block AND receive — Glenn specifically valued Taylor's blocking
Youth and upside — Taylor was only 20 at draft time
Football intelligence and clutch performance — game-winning 2-point conversion vs Alabama
least_important_traits: Elite inline blocking as primary skill — receiving comes first
Size — 251 pounds is average for TE, not seeking oversized TEs
deal_breakers: Inability to catch — hands must be reliable
Pure blocking TE without receiving upside
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Complete skill set (block + receive) with youth and NFL pedigree.

college_production.production_floor: 100+ career receptions preferred for Round 1-2 TE picks. Taylor had 129 career catches, 1,308 yards. Record-setting production at LSU.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values early production and consistency. Taylor started as a true freshman and caught a pass in 28 straight games.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — acknowledged that Taylor competed for targets with Brian Thomas Jr. and Malik Nabers early in career, suppressing numbers.

examples: 2025 R2 No. 42 — Mason Taylor (LSU): 6-5, 251, 4.65 40 (pro day), 32.25-inch arms, 7.06 3-cone, 28 bench. 129 catches, 1,308 yards, 6 TD career. LSU's all-time leader in TE receptions and yards. Son of HOF DE Jason Taylor. Mougey: 'a tight end we've had our eye on.'

TE.confidence_note: 1 TE drafted in Round 2 — strong signal of what they value. Glenn/Mougey clearly prioritized versatile receiving TE.

QB.archetype_summary: Franchise-level passer who can operate in a physical, run-first offense with play-action concepts. Must have mobility to extend plays but primary value is as a passer. Glenn signed Justin Fields in Year 1 free agency — a mobile, strong-armed QB — then later expressed willingness to explore trading up to No. 1 for Fernando Mendoza. Mougey said the Jets 'will exhaust every option' at QB. The Broncos under Paton/Mougey drafted Bo Nix (6-2, 214, R1 2024) — a mobile, accurate, high-processing QB with 61 career college starts. Expect Mougey to value experience, processing speed, mobility, and accuracy.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Not a critical threshold at QB
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and velocity evaluated on film. Mobility matters — Fields and Nix are both mobile QBs. 40 time not critical but must move.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Adequate mobility to extend plays and run designed concepts. Not seeking a pure pocket passer.

playing_style.primary_style: Mobile pocket passer with ability to extend plays and create off-schedule. Must operate within a run-heavy, play-action system (Frank Reich offense).
most_valued_traits: Processing speed and decision-making — Nix had 61 starts; experience matters
Mobility to extend plays and run RPOs — Glenn's offense wanted QB mobility
Accuracy — must be able to execute play-action concepts
Toughness and competitiveness — both Fields and Nix are known competitors
Experience and maturity — multi-year college starters preferred
least_important_traits: Prototype size — willing to take sub-6-2 QBs
Pure arm talent if processing and accuracy are elite
deal_breakers: Pure pocket-only QB without any mobility — doesn't fit the system Glenn envisions
One-year starter with limited experience (higher risk)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Franchise-level QB prospect. Mougey willing to explore massive trade packages (inquiring about No. 1 overall for Mendoza). QB is the position that triggers trade-up behavior.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter strongly preferred. Nix had 61 starts. Fields was a 2-year starter. Experience is a clear priority.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production curve valued. Nix improved dramatically in his final (Oregon) season.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values P5 conference competition and big-game performance.

examples: No QB drafted by Mougey as Jets GM. Signed Justin Fields in FA (6-3, 228, mobile, strong-armed). Broncos drafted Bo Nix R1 (6-2, 214, experienced, mobile, accurate). Mougey expressed interest in trading up for Fernando Mendoza at No. 1 overall in 2026.

QB.confidence_note: No QBs drafted. Profile built from FA signing (Fields), Broncos tree (Nix), and public statements about Mendoza. QB is the single biggest need for the Jets going forward.

RB.archetype_summary: Not a draft priority position. Mougey franchise-tagged Breece Hall rather than drafting an RB. Glenn wants a physical running game but will acquire RBs through free agency and late-round picks rather than early draft capital. Paton's Broncos drafted Javonte Williams in Round 2 (2021) but never used significant capital on RBs again. Expect late-round picks if any: contact-balance, vision-first backs who can handle a primary workload in Frank Reich's run-heavy system.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vision and contact balance evaluated on film. 40 time less important than burst (10-yard split).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Adequate burst to hit holes. Not seeking track-star speed at RB.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Vision, contact balance, and ability to gain yards after contact in a physical run scheme.
most_valued_traits: Vision and instincts — Paton said he valued 'the best vision in the draft' for a Broncos RB pick
Contact balance and yards after contact — physical running style
Pass protection ability — valued in Reich's system
Durability and workload capacity
Receiving ability as additional dimension
least_important_traits: Elite straight-line speed — not seeking home-run hitters necessarily
Explosive big-play ability if it comes at expense of consistency
deal_breakers: Poor pass protection — will get QBs killed in Frank Reich's scheme
Injury history given the position's attrition rate
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Unlikely to reach for any RB. Not a position Mougey values in early rounds.

college_production.production_floor: No clear threshold — may draft low-usage backs who showed flashes.

examples: No RB drafted. Franchise-tagged Breece Hall. Mougey said 'we'll find a way to keep Breece here.' Not a draft priority. Broncos tree: Javonte Williams R2 2021 (5-10, 220, contact balance king), Audric Estime R5 2024 (5-11, 227, power back).

RB.confidence_note: No RBs drafted. Position deprioritized in draft capital. Breece Hall franchise-tagged indicates preference to acquire RBs through other means.

reach_tolerance.notes: Membou at 7 was considered a consensus top-10 pick, so minimal reach. Thomas at 73 was projected between late R1 and early R3 — slightly conservative. Traded up on Day 3 to avoid missing specific targets (Moore, Baron), suggesting willingness to pay to get their guy rather than reaching within their pick slot. Overall approach appears consensus-adjacent in Round 1 but willing to move aggressively on Day 2-3.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Physical, run-first offense with play-action passing under Frank Reich. Zone-run concepts that value athletic, mobile offensive linemen. Glenn preached 'physical, even violent offense that could rush the ball.' Reich's system features power running, play-action, and drop-back passing off it. Values OL versatility.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: 4-3 base with multiple fronts. Glenn calling the defense in 2026. Historically ran league-high levels of man coverage and pressure/blitz packages in Detroit. Nickel is the true base defense. Demands press-man corners, versatile safeties, and EDGE players who can set the edge and rush the passer. Run defense is a stated priority — Glenn's Detroit defenses finished top-5 in rushing yards allowed.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR scheme change for 2026. Year 1 (2025) defensive coordinator Steve Wilks was fired after going 3-14 with historically bad defense (zero interceptions — NFL first). Glenn is now calling the defense personally with Brian Duker as coordinator handling non-playcalling duties. Offensive coordinator changed from Tanner Engstrand to Frank Reich. Entire coaching staff overhauled. The 2025 draft picks were made for a different scheme; 2026 picks will reflect Glenn's direct defensive vision and Reich's offensive system.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk-taker. Conservative in Round 1 (took safe OT over flashy TE) but made traits-over-production bets on Day 3 (Arian Smith's speed despite drops). Blockbuster midseason trades of established stars (Gardner, Williams) show willingness to accept short-term pain for long-term gain. Not reckless but not conservative — 'aggressive but calculated' in his own words.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate recency bias. After a 3-14 season with the NFL's worst defense (zero interceptions), expect heavy defensive investment in 2026 draft. The Jermaine Johnson trade right before the 2026 draft creates immediate EDGE need. QB remains the franchise's biggest void after Justin Fields failed in 2025.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Aggressively moves on from sunk costs. Traded Sauce Gardner 4 months after signing him to a record extension. Traded Quinnen Williams (previous regime's pick) without emotional attachment. Cut Aaron Rodgers after inheriting his contract. Mougey structures deals with trade flexibility built in. This is a 'no sacred cows' GM.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced but leans toward valuing athletic testing at certain positions. Membou's elite combine performance (4.91 at 332 lbs, 99th percentile burst) clearly moved him up the board. Arian Smith's 4.36 40 was the primary selling point. Thomas didn't run the 40 at the combine but had elite film — suggesting film can override testing. Overall: testing confirms film but can also create opportunities (Smith) or non-factors (Thomas).
psychological_profile.character_description: Moderately high character standards. Paton's Broncos emphasized culture and character. Mougey's own stated philosophy: 'acquire, develop, keep our own, and be disciplined.' Drafted team captains (Moore, Membou) and players from strong football families (Taylor — son of HOF Jason Taylor). Glenn values leadership and maturity ('mentality and demeanor' were cited for Membou).
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Desperate to find franchise QB but disciplined about overpaying. Expressed interest in trading up for Mendoza but acknowledged it's unrealistic. Tried Justin Fields in FA — didn't work. Won't reach for a QB just to draft one but will 'exhaust every option.' Willing to use enormous draft capital hoard to trade up for the right QB prospect.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Split. At TE (Taylor: 129 career catches — high production) and LB (Mauigoa: 255 career tackles — high production), picked productive players. At WR (Smith: 48 catches, 10 drops — low production) and EDGE (Baron: 5.5 sacks — moderate production), picked on traits and projection. Tiebreakers likely go to production at premium positions (TE, OT) but traits at non-premium Day 3 positions (WR, EDGE depth).

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No clear floor — Smith had 48 catches with 10 drops. Traits (speed) overrode production concerns.
production_thresholds_by_position.TE: 100+ career receptions and multi-year starter for Round 2 picks. Taylor had 129 career catches.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: 5+ sacks in final season. Baron had 5.5 sacks and 11 TFLs.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear threshold — values advanced coverage metrics over counting stats.
production_thresholds_by_position.OT: Multi-year starter required. 3 seasons at Missouri for Membou.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: 60+ tackles in final season. Mauigoa had 96.

college_production_preferences.notes: Mougey comes from Paton's tree, which valued 'the cleanest player' regardless of positional narrative. Production matters but can be overridden by elite athletic traits (Smith) or elite film grades (Thomas). Context matters significantly — will adjust for scheme, competition, and supporting cast.

CB.notes: Length is premium. Thomas: 6-1.5, 32-3/8 arms. Glenn's man-coverage scheme demands length.

EDGE.notes: Baron: 6-5, 33.125 arms, 4.62. Length and size valued.

OT.notes: Membou: 332, 4.91. Elite athleticism at massive size is the target.

WR.notes: Smith: 4.36. Speed was the defining trait. Small sample.

S.notes: Moore was noted for 'lack of foot speed' — instincts and physicality mattered more.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences at this stage. Only 1 draft of data — insufficient to identify hard cutoffs. OT athleticism appears to be a strong preference (Membou's elite testing). CB length appears to be a strong preference (Thomas's long arms).

size_preferences_by_position.OT: Prefers 320+ pounds with elite movement skills. Membou at 332.
size_preferences_by_position.CB: Prefers 6-0+ with 31.5+ inch arms. Physical, lengthy boundary corners.
size_preferences_by_position.EDGE: Prefers 6-3+ and 250+ with length. Baron at 6-5, 258.
size_preferences_by_position.LB: Flexible — Mauigoa around 6-2, 230. Values range over size.
size_preferences_by_position.WR: Flexible — Smith at 6-0, 179. Speed over size.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Appears to weight broad jump and vertical jump heavily as explosiveness indicators across positions. 40-yard dash is critical for WR and CB evaluations. For OT, the combination of 40 time at weight (Speed Score) is the key metric — Membou's 4.91 at 332 was historic.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first at CB (Thomas didn't run 40 at combine, still drafted Round 3). Testing-influenced at WR (Smith's 4.36 was the selling point despite drop issues on film) and OT (Membou's combine performance solidified his draft position). Overall: film is the base, testing is the tiebreaker — but elite testing can create opportunities that film alone wouldn't.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions, safe picks that address clear needs. Membou at 7 was the 'meat-and-potatoes choice' — the high-floor OL pick over the flashier TE or positional value play. Glenn's 'build from the inside out' mantra drives Round 1 toward trenches and premium defensive positions. In 2026 with picks at No. 2 and No. 16, expect EDGE/DT/QB at 2 and BPA among defense/WR at 16.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: BPA with scheme-fit filter. Taylor at 42 was the best TE on the board and addressed a future need. Thomas at 73 was a scheme-perfect press-man CB. This is where Mougey balances board value with positional need. Willing to take BPA if the value is there (TE wasn't the most pressing need but Taylor was too good to pass).
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Traits-over-production developmental picks and scheme-specific role fillers. Smith (4th round) was a pure traits bet — world-class speed with concerning production. Moore (4th round) was a scheme-fit defensive versatility play. Mauigoa (5th round) was a productive college LB who can contribute on special teams. Baron (5th round) was a long, mature EDGE depth piece. Traded up twice on Day 3, showing willingness to be aggressive for specific targets. Paton's Broncos were known for Day 2-3 value — expect Mougey to try to replicate.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No data yet from Jets tenure. Broncos under Paton used comp picks normally.

OT.note: Membou started immediately at RT. Too early to fully evaluate but promising.

TE.note: Taylor is a rookie — evaluation pending. Draft pick was well-received.

CB.note: Thomas is a rookie — evaluation pending.

WR.note: Smith's drop issues remain a concern.

S.note: Moore played 12 games, showed promise as physical safety.

LB.note: Mauigoa started 8 games after injury to Quincy Williams. 45 tackles. Placed on IR with neck injury.

blind_spots: Too early to identify with only 1 draft. WR evaluation is a concern (traits-over-production bet on Smith who had major drop issues).
strengths: OT evaluation appears strong — Membou was widely praised as a good pick. Broncos tree gives confidence in overall evaluation ability — Paton's drafts produced multiple All-Pros.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Strategic and aggressive when value is right. Had $90M+ in cap space entering 2026. Signed Justin Fields in 2025 FA to address QB (didn't work). Franchise-tagged Breece Hall. Mougey's stated approach: 'aggressive but calculated — knowing your resources, your cap, your future cap.' Plans to use both FA and draft to rebuild.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is acquired at a position, draft capital shifts elsewhere — but not entirely. Signed Fields at QB in 2025 → didn't draft a QB. Tagged Hall at RB → no RB drafted. Expect FA to fill 1-2 major holes (QB, CB?) with draft focused on remaining needs and BPA.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions, supplement through FA. Mougey's stated philosophy: 'draft, develop, keep our own, and be disciplined and strategic in free agency.' The Broncos under Paton built almost entirely through the draft — 13 of 26 starters in 2024 were drafted or signed as UDFAs. Mougey is following the same blueprint with the Jets' massive draft capital stockpile.

notable_patterns: 6 of 7 picks were on offense (2) or defense (5) — no specialists
4 of 7 picks addressed defensive needs (CB, S, LB, EDGE)
First pick was an offensive lineman — 'build from the inside out' philosophy
Traded up twice on Day 3 to target specific players (Moore, Baron)
Every defensive pick fits Glenn's man-heavy, physical defensive identity
Multiple picks were team captains or had football family pedigrees (Taylor, Moore)
Zero trades in Round 1 — stayed put at 7 and took the consensus pick

biggest_reaches.context: Not a significant reach by round — but a traits-over-production bet given 10 drops and limited production.

biggest_steals.consensus_rank: Late R1 to early R2 (projected as high as 23rd overall)
biggest_steals.context: Multiple mocks had Thomas in late Round 1. Fell to 73 — significant value. Did not run 40 at combine, which may have suppressed stock.

confidence_notes: Only 1 draft as Jets GM. Mougey is a first-time GM entering his second draft cycle. His Broncos scouting tree (Elway → Paton) is the primary behavioral model. The 2025 draft provides some signal but the coaching staff overhaul (Wilks → Glenn calling defense; Engstrand → Reich at OC) means the 2026 draft may look meaningfully different in scheme-driven picks. The trade deadline activity (Gardner, Williams blockbusters) provides the strongest behavioral signal about Mougey's decision-making: aggressive, calculated, unsentimental about established players when value is right. Profile should be significantly updated after the 2026 draft.
